Rather that changing the category - I think it might be better to get the same effect by overriding `is_dimmable` in VeraDimmer:-

````python
    @property
    def is_dimmable(self):
        """Device is dimmable."""
        return True
````
That way - once we've decided the object is a Dimmer - other checks will be consistent.

For purity also probably better to refactor the base `VeraDevice` class slightly:-
````python
    @property
    def is_dimmable(self):
        """Default device is not dimmable."""
        return False
````

Longer term I think this would be a better pattern for the others objects too - I will refactor along these lines

The subdag operator passing a flag to the backfill command would be one way to do it. Another would be to make the subdag operator mutate the subdag's max active dag runs. Personally I think subdags should be a first class citizen in airflow (as opposed to being an operator that forks a separate process), but that is for another time.

I think that making the backfill use the scheduler is definitely the place we want to end up. One problem I can see for existing airflow use cases is that it would make it hard to iterate on/test dags since you could not pass a local copy of a dag into a backfill. Once this problem is solved I can't think of any other blockers (at least for AIrbnb).

Note that this end-goal can already be achieved by using the same executor in the airflow.cfg on the backfill machine as the scheduler uses.

I pushed a new temporary commit using `BrotliEncoderTakeOutput`, but I'm getting errors when trying to execute sequences of operations.

Here are some scenarios:
```
process("one two three ")
finish()
```
**success**

```
process("one ")
process("two ")
process("three ")
finish()
```
**success**

```
process("one ")
process("two ")
flush()
process("three ")
```
**failure:** `BrotliEncoderCompressStream` returns `false`

```
process("one ")
process("two ")
process("three ")
flush()
finish()
```
**failure:** `BrotliEncoderIsFinished` returns `false` and output is incomplete

So it seems to me that either
1. something with `BrotliEncoderCompressStream(..., BROTLI_OPERATION_FLUSH, ...)` and `BrotliEncoderTakeOutput` is not working correctly
2. I'm calling `BrotliEncoderCompressStream` incorrectly now.

Are we sure these are the only date formats we want to match?  Looking at the `date MonthName yyyy` format it occurs to me that when I say my birthday I usually use the format `MonthName date yyyy` - should we be matching for this as well?

Also - the comments say these match on dates formatted with dashes (`-`) but they will also match date formatted with forward slashes(`/`) but not back slashes (`\`).  I'm not sure if it matters, but these will match across line breaks as well.

In summary, these will match:
`11/11/1111`
```
11-
11-
1111
```

and these won't:
`11\11\1111`

Will this be ok in our situation?

Ah, so while previously we allowed a process to wait for the whole loop_wait after calling recover (which was ultimately calling state_handler.follow asynchronously, therefore, had to wait for at least loop_wait afterwards), with this patch the loop will be terminated as soon as the follow will return True, which could be faster than loop_wait (but not necessary), therefore, starting much faster and giving less time for the other cluster members to start. Ultimately, I think this doesn't really give us any guarantees (it could happen that the first 2 nodes to start would be the "slowest" ones) and makes the whole block harder to comprehend, so I vote for removing it.

It is indeed these changes which is now causing the test suite to run a lot slower. If it's fixing things then I agree that it's not itself a problem as 'correct' beats 'fast'. Two things though. Firstly, I believe that:
```python
for i in ThreadPool:
    del i
del ThreadPool[:]
ThreadPool = []
```
could all just be replaced with
```python
del ThreadPool[:]
```

The second and most important thing to realise here though is that just because the threads have been 'deleted' (had this reference removed) that doesn't mean that they will stop running. If one if them was doing something which is hanging (maybe talking to DIRAC is taking many minutes) then running this code will not halt that process, it will continue to run in the background. This could cause situations where we have two DIRAC monitoring threads running simultaneously.

In Python there is essentially no way to kill a thread dead if it's busy. The only safe thing to do in reality is to wait for the threads to to finish.

This method raises an error crashing Certbot if the requested enhancement is not supported. I do not think we should do this here. This code will not run until near the very end of Certbot's execution so unfortunately this doesn't make Certbot fail fast.

Instead, I think we should:

1. Change `raise error.Error(msg)` in `_verify_all_config_options_supported` to `logger.warning(msg)`.
2. Call `_very_all_config_options_supported` in this class's `__init__` method. This will make Certbot fail fast as this happens before Certbot has begun any communication with Let's Encrypt
